checkpoints.trigger: before implementing approve() behavior for non-high appraisal confidence
checkpoints.question: The approve gate is the pipeline's only quality check, and appraisals now carry {id, value} confidence of high|medium|low|unknown (absent = unknown). Morley's stated rule: 'I'd rather I-don't-know than wrong.' When Morley approves a listing whose confidence is not high/high, what should approve() do? Options: (a) WARN — record confidence in the approval jsonb and print a prominent warning line, but approve normally (fastest flow, but a warning in CLI output is easy to scroll past, and the dashboard approve path may not show stdout at all); (b) REQUIRE-ACK — approve() raises unless called with an explicit acknowledge_low_confidence=True (CLI flag --ack-low-confidence), forcing a conscious step; the ack is recorded in approval jsonb (one extra step only on flagged items, none on clean ones); (c) BLOCK below a floor — unknown/unknown or authenticity-flagged items cannot be approved until re-appraised (strongest protection, but Morley may legitimately want to sell an honestly-unknown item described as such). Which behavior, given the human IS the gate and the goal is preventing accidental approval of uncertain appraisals, not preventing deliberate ones?
checkpoints.on_synthesis: implement exactly the chosen behavior in approve() and its CLI surface, record confidence + any acknowledgment in the approval jsonb, and cover both the flagged and clean paths in tests
